questions.text: A written document in which we find the rules by which a society is governed is called a:
questions.explanation: Most modern countries have a written document that defines the rules and principles of their governance, known as a Constitution.

questions.text: In which year did the Indian National Congress make the demand for a Constituent Assembly?
questions.explanation: The demand for a Constituent Assembly was first made by the INC in 1934.

questions.text: The Constituent Assembly met for the first time in:
questions.explanation: Between December 1946 and November 1949, the Constituent Assembly drafted a constitution for independent India.

questions.text: Who is known as the 'Father of the Indian Constitution'?
questions.explanation: Dr. Ambedkar was the Chairman of the Drafting Committee and played a key role in framing the constitution.

questions.text: Nepal adopted an interim Constitution in 2007 because:
options: It moved from a Monarchy to a Democracy
questions.explanation: Nepal's previous constitution reflected the power of the King; a new one was needed to reflect democratic ideals after the people's movement.

questions.text: The 'tyranny of the majority' refers to:
options: A small group making decisions for all
The majority group excluding or dominating the minority
questions.explanation: The Constitution ensures that the majority does not use its power to violate the rights of minority groups.

questions.text: Which of the following is a key feature of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Constitution includes several pillars like Federalism, Parliamentary form, Separation of Powers, Fundamental Rights, and Secularism.

questions.text: Federalism refers to:
options: One central government for the whole country
Existence of more than one level of government
questions.explanation: In India, we have governments at the Center, the States, and the Panchayati Raj level.

questions.text: In a Parliamentary form of government, who has the right to vote?
questions.explanation: The Constitution guarantees universal adult suffrage for all citizens.

questions.text: The three organs of the State as per the Constitution are:
questions.explanation: Legislature makes laws, Executive implements them, and Judiciary interprets them.

questions.text: The 'Executive' is a group of people who are responsible for:
options: Implementing laws and running the government
questions.explanation: The Executive is the branch of government that carries out the laws.

questions.text: Fundamental Rights are often referred to as the ________ of the Indian Constitution.
questions.explanation: Fundamental rights protect citizens against the arbitrary exercise of power by the State.

questions.text: Which Fundamental Right allows citizens to move the court if they believe their rights have been violated?
questions.explanation: This right allows citizens to approach the Supreme Court or High Courts to protect their other fundamental rights.

questions.text: Secularism means that the State:
options: Does not officially promote any one religion
questions.explanation: A secular state maintains a principled distance from all religions.

questions.text: Right against Exploitation prohibits:
questions.explanation: Articles 23 and 24 protect individuals from trafficking, forced labour, and employment of children under 14.

questions.text: The Right to Freedom of Religion includes the right to:
questions.explanation: Every person has the right to profess, practice and propagate the religion of their choice.

questions.text: What is the role of the 'Judiciary'?
options: To settle disputes and uphold the law
questions.explanation: The Judiciary system (courts) ensures that the laws of the land are followed.

questions.text: The Constitution of India was adopted on ________ and came into effect on ________.
questions.explanation: It was completed in Nov 1949 but officially implemented on Jan 26, 1950, to celebrate Republic Day.

questions.text: Why do we need a Constitution?
options: To define the nature of our political system
To provide safeguards against powerful leaders
To protect the rights of minorities
questions.explanation: A constitution serves many purposes, including setting ideals and protecting citizens.

questions.text: Untouchability is abolished under which Fundamental Right?
questions.explanation: Right to Equality includes the abolition of untouchability and discrimination based on caste.

questions.text: Which organ of the state consists of our elected representatives?
questions.explanation: The legislature (Parliament/State Assemblies) is where laws are made by representatives.

questions.text: A 'Polity' refers to a society that has a ________.
questions.explanation: A polity is a politically organized body or system.

questions.text: Which fundamental right ensures that all persons are equal before the law?
questions.explanation: Equality before the law means everyone is subject to the same laws regardless of status.

questions.text: The Directive Principles of State Policy were included to:
options: Ensure more social and economic reforms
Give more power to the police
questions.explanation: They serve as a guide to the independent Indian State to institute laws that reduce poverty and improve society.

questions.text: Under the Right to Freedom, citizens have the right to:
questions.explanation: The Right to Freedom includes several liberties vital for a democracy.

questions.text: Human trafficking means:
questions.explanation: Human trafficking is the practice of buying and selling humans for exploitation.

questions.text: The Constitution protects us from ________.
options: Ourselves (making wrong decisions that hurt principles)
questions.explanation: A constitution helps protect us from making decisions that might go against our larger interests or values.

questions.text: What is the age for Universal Adult Suffrage in India?
questions.explanation: Every citizen aged 18 or above has the right to vote.

questions.text: Which right ensures that minorities can preserve their language and culture?
questions.explanation: These rights allow religious and linguistic minorities to set up their own educational institutions.

questions.text: The 'Drafting Committee' of the Constitution was headed by:
questions.explanation: Dr. Ambedkar was the chief architect and chairman of this critical committee.

questions.text: Constituent Assembly members were ________.
questions.explanation: They were a group of around 300 people who spent 3 years writing the document.

questions.text: Fundamental Rights are ________ in nature.
questions.explanation: If these rights are violated, a person can go to court.

questions.text: India is a ________ country.
questions.explanation: India has a system of government by the whole population through elected representatives.

questions.text: Freedom from forced labour is under which right?
questions.explanation: The Constitution prohibits 'begar' or forced labour.

questions.text: The 'Separation of Powers' prevents:
options: The misuse of power by any one branch of government
questions.explanation: It creates a system of checks and balances between the Legislature, Executive, and Judiciary.

questions.text: Which right was described by Dr. Ambedkar as the 'Soul of the Constitution'?
questions.explanation: He considered it the most important because it makes all other rights effective.

questions.text: In a democracy, we choose our leaders so that:
options: They can do whatever they want
They can exercise power responsibly on our behalf
They can stay in power forever
questions.explanation: Leaders are representatives of the people and are accountable to the constitution.

questions.text: The first President of Independent India was:
questions.explanation: Dr. Rajendra Prasad served as the first President and was also the President of the Constituent Assembly.

questions.text: Sovereignty means:
questions.explanation: India is a sovereign nation, meaning it is independent and has its own supreme authority.

questions.text: The Preamble is the ________ to the Constitution.
questions.explanation: The Preamble outlines the main objectives and philosophy of the Constitution.
